---
number-sections: true
number-offset: [10, 0]  # This makes sections 10.1, 10.2, etc.
---
# (Gradient) Boosting

## Exercises

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise 11.1: First Step of Gradient Boosting (Regression)

You observe four data points:  
$$
(x_i, y_i) = (1,2), (2,3), (3,2.5), (4,5)
$$

Assume squared error loss and decision stumps as weak learners (one split). No learning rate (i.e. $\nu = 1$).

(i) Briefly state the main idea of (gradient) boosting in regression.  
(ii) Compute the initial model $f_0(x)$, residuals $r_i$, fit the optimal first stump $h_1(x)$ (try split points at midpoints 1.5, 2.5, 3.5), and write the updated model $f_1(x)=f_0(x)+h_1(x)$.

<span style="color:blue">Please note the following: In the lecture, I presented the boosting algorithm with $F_0(x) = 0$ in line with what you can find in ISL. However, most implementations (like `sklearn`) use the mean $\bar y$ as initial model. My solution below will use this more widespread initial model.<span>

*Exam level*
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
### Hint 1 (Initial Model)
For squared error loss the best constant is the sample mean of $y$.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
### Hint 2 (Residual Stump)
For each candidate split compute mean residual left/right → choose split minimizing residual SSE.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
### Hint 3 (Update)
With $\nu=1$: $F_1 = F_0 + h_1$. If a learning rate $\nu<1$ were used you would scale \(h_1\).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
### Solution

(i) Boosting builds an additive model $f_B(x)=\sum_{b=0}^B h_b(x)$ where each new weak learner fits (an approximation to) the negative gradient of the loss—under squared error this is the residual.

(ii)  
$$
f_0(x)=\bar y=\tfrac{2+3+2.5+5}{4}=3.125
$$
Residuals:
$$
r=(-1.125,\,-0.125,\,-0.625,\,1.875)
$$
Candidate split points: 1.5, 2.5, 3.5. Optimal: \(x=3.5\) (groups: first three vs last).

Region means:
$$
\bar r_{x\le 3.5}=\tfrac{-1.125-0.125-0.625}{3}=-0.625,\qquad \bar r_{x>3.5}=1.875
$$
So
$$
h_1(x)=\begin{cases}-0.625 & x\le 3.5\\ 1.875 & x>3.5\end{cases}
$$
Update:
$$
f_1(x)=f_0(x)+h_1(x)=
\begin{cases}
3.125-0.625=2.5 & x\le 3.5\\
3.125+1.875=5.0 & x>3.5
\end{cases}
$$
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## Beyond squared error: cross-entropy loss for classification boosting

The exercise above uses squared error loss, where the negative gradient is simply the residual. For **classification** problems, gradient boosting typically uses the **cross-entropy (log-likelihood) loss**:
$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^n \left[y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\right]
$$
where $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(f(x_i))$ is the predicted probability via the sigmoid function. The negative gradient with respect to $f(x_i)$ is $y_i - \hat{p}_i$ — similar in form to the residual but now operating on the probability scale. This connects directly to the [Information Theory chapter](information_theory.qmd#sec-mle-kl-divergence): minimizing cross-entropy loss is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence between the data distribution and the model's predicted distribution. Popular implementations such as XGBoost and LightGBM use this as their default classification objective.
:::
